title: Renting a Boat in Corsica: A Practical Guide to the Île de Beauté by Sea
html:
Corsica earns its nickname, the Île de Beauté, the moment you see it from the water. Granite cliffs plunge into turquoise coves, hidden beaches sit beyond the reach of any coastal road, and the light has a clarity that photographers chase for years. The simplest way to reach the best of it is to rent a boat and explore at your own pace, slipping into anchorages that day-trippers never see.

Why Corsica suits a boat trip

The Corsican coastline runs for roughly a thousand kilometres, and a remarkable share of its finest stretches are only reachable by sea. The Scandola Nature Reserve, a UNESCO-listed maze of red volcanic rock, allows no road access at all. The Lavezzi Islands off the southern tip are a scatter of smooth granite and clear shallows that feel closer to the tropics than the Mediterranean. A boat turns these from postcards into a genuine afternoon.

Distances between highlights are short, which makes the island forgiving for those new to time on the water. You can wake in one bay, motor an hour to lunch in another, and still have the afternoon for swimming. That rhythm is hard to match on land, where the mountain roads are slow and the best coves stay stubbornly out of reach.

Choosing the right vessel

Your ideal craft depends on how far you want to roam and how many people are coming along.

  • Small open boats and RIBs are perfect for a single day. Many require no licence under a certain engine size, which makes them ideal for couples or small families wanting a few hours of independence.
  • Motor cruisers suit groups who want speed and shade, letting you cover Ajaccio to the Gulf of Valinco comfortably between breakfast and sunset.
  • Sailing yachts and catamarans reward anyone planning several days aboard, with room to sleep, cook and live at anchor.

If you are unsure where to start, browse the listings, compare specifications and read what previous renters have said. It is easy to hire a boat that genuinely matches your plans rather than settling for whatever is left at the dock.

Where to launch and where to point the bow

Corsica gives you several natural bases, each opening a different corner of the island.

The south: Bonifacio and Porto-Vecchio

Bonifacio, perched on white limestone cliffs above a fjord-like harbour, is the classic departure point for the Lavezzi and Cavallo islands. Porto-Vecchio, a short hop north, fronts a string of celebrated beaches such as Palombaggia and Santa Giulia. This is the warmest, most sheltered region and a sensible choice for first-timers.

The west: Ajaccio, Porto and Scandola

The western gulfs offer the island's most dramatic scenery. From Ajaccio or the village of Porto you can reach the Calanques de Piana and the Scandola reserve, where ochre rock meets deep blue water and ospreys wheel overhead.

The north: Calvi and the Balagne

Calvi pairs a long sandy bay with a Genoese citadel, and the surrounding Balagne coast hides quiet creeks ideal for an unhurried swim. Whichever base appeals, you can find a boat nearby and tailor the route to the wind.

When to go and what it costs

The season runs from late spring to early autumn. June and September are the sweet spot: the sea is warm, the crowds have thinned, and prices ease back from their August peak. July and August bring the liveliest atmosphere but also the highest demand, so booking well ahead is wise.

Costs vary widely with size, season and whether a skipper is included. A small day boat is an affordable treat for two, while a crewed yacht for a week is a holiday in itself. Comparing options across a single boat rental marketplace makes it far easier to see where your budget stretches furthest.

The Mediterranean can turn quickly. Always check the local forecast, respect the mistral wind, and never anchor in a marine reserve where mooring is restricted.

Practical tips before you cast off

  1. Confirm what licence, if any, your chosen boat needs, and carry your documents aboard.
  2. Pack sun protection, water and soft-soled shoes; granite landings can be sharp.
  3. Note that fuel is usually charged separately, so ask before you book.
  4. Download an offline chart, as phone signal fades in the remoter gulfs.

Corsica rewards those who plan a little and then let the coast lead. If you own a vessel here and would like it earning between trips, you can also list your boat and reach travellers from around the world. Either way, the island is at its best from the water, and getting out there has never been simpler.
